_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve graphs Treasury yields across maturities, typically ranging from 1 month to 30 years. A normal yield curve has an upward slope, reflecting higher yields for longer maturities due to risks like inflation and time value. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.54% with a 10-year minus 2-year spread (2s10s) at 0.35 points, indicating a normal, upward slope. This contrasts with the 2022-2024 period when the curve was inverted (2s10s below zero points), a historically rare condition where short-term rates exceeded long-term rates.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread is a key recession indicator, as an inverted yield curve—when this spread is negative—has preceded every modern U.S. recession by about 6 to 18 months. During 2022 through 2024, this spread was inverted but has since returned above zero to 0.35 points, reflecting a re-steepening of the curve. The NY Fed recession probability currently stands at 31%, a notable but not extreme level of recession risk. The historical caveat is that the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before recession onset, so an until-now inversion followed by steepening does not exclude recession risks immediately ahead.
notes.now: The yield curve is presently normal with a positive 2s10s spread, suggesting reduced near-term recession probability compared to the inversion period. Nonetheless, a NY Fed recession probability of 31% indicates meaningful risk remains, warranting close monitoring of yield curve dynamics without assuming recession timing.
